title: Cirrus: Make efficient and performant
body:
Reimplement CI-automation to remove accumulated technical-debt and
optimize workflow.  The task-dependency graph designed goal was to
shorten it's depth and increase width (i.e. more parallelism).  A
reduction in redundant building (and 3rd party module download) was
also realized by caching `$GOPATH` and `$GOCACHE` early on.  This
cache is then reused in favor of a fresh clone of the repository
(when possible).

Note: The system tests typically execute MUCH faster than the
integration tests.  However, contrary to a fail-fast/fail-early
principal, they are executed last.  This was implemented due to
debug-ability related concerns/preferences of the primary
(golang-centric) project developers.

Signed-off-by: Chris Evich <cevich@redhat.com>

comments.body:

(You may want to [read up on YAML anchors/aliases first](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/tips-and-tricks/#sharing-configuration-between-tasks))

That's exactly what I meant: all that work is gone. Or at least most of it. 15f273b93

```
* gating Expected - Waiting for status to be reported  (Required)
```
(See attached screenshot)
![gating-missing](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/265639/86175824-3b171d80-bae1-11ea-85a3-001cfce5846b.png)

What am I missing?

BTW there's no way I can review this all in one day; I'll continue tomorrow

@edsantiago thanks for looking.  

> all that work is gone. Or at least most of it.

Aww :cry: Are you sure the `name` changes are necessary?  What you learned about ssh and envvar passing seems **MUCH** more significant.  Maybe the problem you're hitting with it is simply the nested `env` -> `matrix` -> `env` blocks...they don't look valid to me (or maybe I'm seeing things).  Is there a PR I can look at?

> In the github PR status page, I see:

Ignore those. They're added by github due to repository-wide settings we have enabled.  We'll address those when this PR is closer to merging or merged.

> In the diffs I'm looking at, I see gating removed. In the github PR status page, I see:

Sorry, I should make that more clear in the description.  This change is in a comment but easily missed: I'm proposing we rename 'gate' -> 'smoke' for several reasons, but primarily so it doesn't cause confusion with the `gating` term used in downstream testing (i.e. RHEL and Fedora modules / dist-git).

For reference/background, the dependency-graph of the tasks here is based on discussions Brent and I had.  The central idea is to run as much in parallel as possible, counting on CIrrus-CI canceling everything upon the first failure (since `success` task depends on everything passing).  Also note: I'm planning to move all VM and container image building code and configuration into a dedicated repository (so no more multi-mode runtime magic, like `CI:IMG` or `CI:DOC`).

> I can review this all in one day; I'll continue tomorrow

Please take your time, this is only a prototype for a much larger piece of work I'm **not** eager to rush through :grin:

**Major problems:** With current container & VM image build workflow

1. _Sometimes_ I read failure notification mails, and go check if there's at least one green build for each branch, on each repo.  Most of the time I can't be bothered.
2. When something breaks, I scramble to decode what went wrong in the past (sometimes with limited data), and somewhat blindly change the images so they seem to work.
3. When builds are working okay images are constantly updated on quay.io.  Depending on timing, this can mean somebody's in-flight tests on a PR pull and execute different images, possibly with different behavior, and very little observe-ability.
4. When someone wants to change a container image, they have to do so locally, push with a "test" tag, and fudge a PR to use that image.  Assuming testing passes, they merge the PR, wait for quay.io to build+tag the image and hope nobody's in-flight tests break.
5. There's a constantly changing 'latest' tag, which may come from a build on a branch nobody cares about currently.  It's super-duper easy to pull and try to use `latest` (for local testing) outside the context it was built for.  i.e. "latest" doesn't actually mean latest in all environment-contexts.

-----

What I propose is a separate repository with:

1. A standalone CI/CD setup, but authorized with the same google-cloud and quay.io services.
1. Separate branches that pair with dependent repositories and their release branches.  e.g. libpod-v1.6, buildah-v1.14, etc.
2. The standard PR -> Merge workflow is used on each branch where:
    1. We build VM and container images (including in_podman_fedora, gate, imgts, etc.) in every PR, and (maybe) periodically on each branch.
    2. We run repository specific smoke tests against the produced images.  This will mainly be very simplistic, like running 'make all' and checking the exit status.  Assuming all is well, the container images are tagged and pushed to quay.io.  The VM images simply stay where they are (we use the same imgts + imgprune schemes to maintain "current" images).
    3.  With the images-repo PR still open, a PR is opened in the dependent repo. referencing the just produced/pushed  named/numbered/tagged images.
    4. Regular/Full CI tests run for the dependent that repo, validating the soundness of the new image for it's purpose.
    5. If tests fail, adjustments are made in their respective repository PRs, and life goes on.  If everything looks good, the PRs are merged (order doesn't matter), and life goes on.
3. Independently of any open image PRs, all dependent repos. continue using their defined tagged/numbered images as normal, without interruption or surprises.
5. Once the image and dependent repo. PRs merge, other PRs need to rebase to use the new images, just as it is today, further limiting developer surprises.

/approve

We talked about this in Watercooler this week. There's no way to really review it. We just have to take the plunge. I will look at it over the next two hours and give it my best shot, but unfortunately I will be net-unavailable all of tomorrow.

> We talked about this in Watercooler this week. There's no way to really review it. We just have to take the plunge. I will look at it over the next two hours and give it my best shot, but unfortunately I will be net-unavailable all of tomorrow.

Ya, it's a beast, I completely understand, and would greatly appreciate even 30-minutes of your eyeball time :grin: 

Thanks
@edsantiago BTW...for my part, I'm going through and writing up descriptions/comments for each task in .cirrus.yml  I'll keep my hands off of the scripts until tomorrow.

First pass finished (on scripts; I didn't even look at `.cirrus.yml`). It LGTM. Some nits, but nothing I'm even going to bother mentioning. They can be fine-tuned over time. The important thing is that CI passes (mostly) and that it seems to be quicker than our current method. I think we all want to see this go live.
(Deleted my earlier comment. Never mind. Let's just get this in, then all of us as a team can take care of making future changes to the parts that affect us)
